Full statement: weight of index constituents: max of weight in parent (cap-weighted) index + 2% or 10 times weight in parent index; sector weights: restricted to 5% deviation vs parent; exposure to style factors (growth, liquidity): restricted to 0.25 SD from parent; limit on volatility: restricted to 0.25 SD from parent.

Correct. Per Exhibit 19 (Sample Investment Policy Risk Constraints): 'The MSCI Diversified Multi-Factor Index uses an optimization process to maximize the exposure score to several risk factors. The index seeks to achieve this objective while controlling for several portfolio and risk characteristics, such as the following: Weight of index constituents: maximum of weight in the parent (capitalization-weighted) index + 2% or 10 times weight in the parent index. Sector weights: restricted to a 5% deviation against the parent index. Exposure to style factors, such as growth and liquidity: restricted to a 0.25 standard deviation from the parent index. Limit on volatility: restricted to a 0.25 standard deviation from the parent index.'

questions.stem: The *consequences of being wrong about risk* example (leveraged mortgage-related securities) *most likely*:

options.A: in 2008 hedge fund owned 2× levered portfolio of highly-rated mortgage-related securities.
options.B: trivial.
options.C: no example.

rationales.A:
Full statement: in 2008 hedge fund owned 2× levered portfolio of highly-rated mortgage-related securities; although specific securities not materially exposed to subprime, concerns about economy and poor market liquidity led to steep decline; prices quickly recovered but presence of 2× leverage combined with unprecedented decline led to forced liquidation just days before recovery; manager and investors lost all capital.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The consequences of being wrong about risk expectations can be significant but even more so when a strategy is leveraged. In 2008, for example, a hedge fund owned a two-times levered portfolio of highly rated mortgage-related securities. Although the specific securities were not materially exposed to subprime mortgages, concerns about the economy and poor market liquidity led to a steep decline in the prices of these securities. Prices quickly recovered, but the presence of the 2× leverage combined with an unprecedented price decline led to a forced liquidation of the assets just a few days before prices recovered. The manager and his investors lost all capital.'
rationales.B: Real disaster.
rationales.C: Cautionary tale.

options.A: Exhibit 20 shows S&P 500 volatility constant across 1995–2020.
options.B: volatility remains in range of 10%-20% most of the time but periods of much higher volatility observed: 2000-2002 (technology stocks collapse), 2008-2009 (Global Financial Crisis), 2020 (COVID-19 pandemic).
options.C: The exhibit shows no variation in realized volatility over the period.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Volatility is regime-dependent — a 10%–20% normal range punctuated by spikes (2000–2002 tech collapse, 2008–2009 crisis, 2020 pandemic).
rationales.B:
Full statement: volatility remains in range of 10%-20% most of the time but periods of much higher volatility observed: 2000-2002 (technology stocks collapse), 2008-2009 (Global Financial Crisis), 2020 (COVID-19 pandemic); volatility spikes can be sector specific (tech in early 2000s, energy in 2014-2015); some managers tighten risk constraints in more volatile periods.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Exhibit 20 illustrates the time-varying volatility of the S&P 500 from 1995-2020. Although volatility remains in a range of 10%-20% most of the time, periods of much higher volatility are observed: in 2000-2002 when technology stocks collapsed, in the 2008-2009 the Global Financial Crisis, and in 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic. Effective risk management requires the manager to account for the fact that unexpected volatility can derail the investment strategy. Furthermore, spikes in volatility can also be sector specific — the technology sector in the early 2000s and the energy sector in 2014 and 2015. Therefore, what may seem to be an acceptable sector deviation limit in normal times may be the source of significant active losses in a different environment. Some managers may tighten risk constraints in more volatile periods to protect the portfolio against excessive variability.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The variation is the exhibit's point: long calm stretches interrupted by episodes of far higher volatility.

drills.los_text: discuss how assets under management, position size, market liquidity, and portfolio turnover affect equity portfolio construction decisions

options.A: no cost.
options.B: no concept.
options.C: price movement resulting from manager's purchase or sale of a security can materially erode a manager's alpha.

rationales.A: Alpha-eroding cost.
rationales.B: Real cost.
rationales.C:
Full statement: price movement resulting from manager's purchase or sale of a security can materially erode a manager's alpha; function of the liquidity and trade size of the security; manager's investment approach and style influences extent of exposure to market impact costs; strategy demanding immediacy in execution or requiring higher portfolio turnover likely incurs higher market impact costs than manager who patiently trades into a position.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The price movement (or market impact) resulting from a manager's purchase or sale of a security can materially erode a manager's alpha. Market impact is a function of the liquidity and trade size of the security. A manager's investment approach and style will influence the extent to which he is exposed to market impact costs. A manager whose strategy demands immediacy in execution or requires a higher portfolio turnover is likely to incur higher market impact costs relative to a manager who patiently trades into a position. A manager who believes her investment insights will be rewarded over a longer-term investment horizon may be able to mitigate market impact costs by slowly building up positions as liquidity becomes available.'

options.A: when discretionary manager with sizable AUM begins to buy a stock, trade signals to other market participants likely upward pressure as manager builds position.
options.B: Trades by large discretionary managers convey no signal to other market participants.
options.C: Information-containing trades have no effect on execution prices.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when discretionary manager with sizable AUM begins to buy a stock, trade signals to other market participants likely upward pressure as manager builds position; some may 'front-run' the manager buying up supply to sell at higher price; if same manager begins to sell following company 'event', signals view has changed and manager likely selling off putting downward pressure on price.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A manager whose trades contain "information" is more vulnerable to market impact costs. A trade contains information when the manager's decision to buy or sell the security signals to the market that something has changed. If a discretionary manager with sizable assets under management begins to buy a stock, the trade signals to other market participants that there is likely to be upward pressure on the stock price as the manager builds the position. Some market participants may try to "front-run" the manager, buying up known supply to sell it to the manager at a higher price. If that same manager begins to sell his position following a company "event," it signals to the market that the manager's view on the stock has changed and he is likely to be selling off his position, putting downward pressure on the price.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Visible accumulation by a sizable informed manager signals likely upward pressure — others react.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The signaling raises the cost of completing the position — prices move against the informed trader.

options.A: no pattern.
options.B: (1) distribution of market cap is skewed — average capitalization declines quickly.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A: Two observations.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) distribution of market cap is skewed — average capitalization declines quickly; combined capitalization of top 500 companies is more than seven times that of the bottom 500 companies; (2) smaller-cap companies have lower daily trading volume (in dollars) but trade greater percentage of their capitalization — smallest 900 within index trade nearly 2× more volume as % of cap than 100 largest (900 smallest average 1% of market cap daily vs 100 largest 0.5%).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Two observations are warranted. First, the distribution of market cap is skewed: The average capitalization declines quickly. The combined capitalization of the top 500 companies is more than seven times that of the bottom 500 companies. Second, smaller-capitalization companies have lower daily trading volume (in dollars). However, smaller-cap companies trade a greater percentage of their capitalization. The smallest 900 companies within the index trade nearly two times more volume — as a percentage of their market capitalization — than the 100 largest companies (e.g., the 900 smallest companies on average trade 1% of their market cap daily, whereas the 100 largest companies trade 0.5% of their market cap daily). Nevertheless, the lower absolute level of average trading volume of the smaller securities can be a significant implementation hurdle for a manager running a strategy with significant assets under management and significant positive active weights on smaller companies.'
rationales.C: Real observations.

questions.stem: *Worked example* — smallest company $2B cap, 1% daily trades ($20M avg), 10% liquidity policy, 2% position limit *most likely*:

options.A: The worked example shows the position is unconstrained by either policy.
options.B: The example poses the liquidity question but performs no arithmetic.
options.C: if manager has $200M AUM, allocation constraint says he could own as much as $4M of that security ($200M × 2% = $4M) but liquidity constraint limits position to $2M ($20M × 10%).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The binding constraint emerges from the math — the liquidity policy (10% of $20M daily volume = $2M) binds before the 2% allocation cap ($4M).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The computation is explicit: $200M × 2% = $4M allocation cap versus the tighter $2M liquidity cap.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if manager has $200M AUM, allocation constraint says he could own as much as $4M of that security ($200M × 2% = $4M) but liquidity constraint limits position to $2M ($20M × 10%); thus position limited to about 1.0% of fund assets; $1B fund with similar constraints limited to same $2M position, much smaller relative position size.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, let's assume the smallest company within an index has a capitalization of $2 billion and that 1% of its capitalization trades each day on average — about $20 million. Let's also assume that a manager has a policy not to own a position that constitutes more than 10% of the average trading volume of a security and that no position in the portfolio can be larger than 2% of total assets. If this manager has $200 million under management, the allocation constraint indicates that he could own as much as $4 million of that security ($200 million × 2% = $4 million), but the liquidity constraint limits the position to $2 million ($20 million × 10%). Thus, the position size is limited to about 1.0% of the fund's assets. A $1 billion fund with similar constraints would be limited to the same $2 million position, a much smaller position size relative to his total portfolio.'

options.A: (1) may establish position limits on individual securities that consider respective market-cap weights on absolute and relative basis (e.g., lesser of market-cap weight + 1% or 10× market-cap weight).
options.B: Large-AUM funds have no available mechanisms for managing liquidity constraints.
options.C: The only remedy for a large fund's liquidity constraint is closing the fund to new investors.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) may establish position limits on individual securities that consider respective market-cap weights on absolute and relative basis (e.g., lesser of market-cap weight + 1% or 10× market-cap weight); (2) may establish position limits based on average daily trading volume (e.g., no more than 10 days of avg trading volume); (3) may build rebalancing strategy anticipating longer rebalancing period or gradually rebalancing over time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A large-AUM fund can address this issue in several ways: It may establish position limits on individual securities that consider their respective market-cap weights on both an absolute and relative basis. For example, it may limit the allocation to the lesser of market-cap weight + 1% (100 bps) or 10 times the market-cap weight allocation of the security within the index. In other words, the position limit would be related to the market cap of each security. It may establish position limits based on the average daily trading volume of a security. For example, it may limit the position size to, say, no more than 10 days of average trading volume. It may build a rebalancing strategy into the investment process that anticipates a longer rebalancing period or that gradually and consistently rebalances over time, assuming the performance of the strategy is not affected by the implementation delay.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Position limits scaled to market cap and volume, liquidity-aware sizing, and universe adjustments are all available.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Closure is one lever among several; structural position-limit policies are the primary tools described.

options.A: Small-cap funds face no distinctive implementation issues.
options.B: weighted average capitalization of Russell 2000 is only $2.2 billion, and nearly 60% of companies in index have market cap below $1B.
options.C: Small-cap benchmarks contain no companies with limited market capitalization.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Capacity and liquidity constraints bite hard in small-cap: the Russell 2000's weighted average cap is only about $2.2B.
rationales.B:
Full statement: weighted average capitalization of Russell 2000 is only $2.2 billion, and nearly 60% of companies in index have market cap below $1B; strategy rooted in smaller number of securities (say 40) may find difficult to run $100M fund and may have to concentrate allocation among 25% largest securities or accept lower turnover; small-cap funds with capacities of $1B or greater may need to hold 400 securities or more.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The challenges are even greater for small-cap funds. The weighted average capitalization of the Russell 2000 Index is only $2.2 billion, and nearly 60% of the companies in the index have a market capitalization below $1 billion (as of March 2017)... A small-cap manager with the same limits on position size relative to trading volume as the manager above would have an average position size of no more than $2.5 million, based on average daily trading volume. A strategy rooted in a smaller number of securities — say, 40 — may find it difficult to run a $100 million fund and may have to concentrate its allocation among the 25% largest securities in the index or accept a lower turnover. Although a strategy with a larger number of securities — say, 200 — would be able to support a substantially higher level of AUM, it may still be constrained to concentrate its exposure among the larger and more liquid securities. Small-cap funds with capacities of $1 billion or greater may very well need to hold 400 securities or more.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Nearly 60% of Russell 2000 constituents fall below $1B in market cap — the structural challenge itself.

options.A: Slippage is not a measurable trading cost.
options.B: Slippage is unrelated to the prices at which trades execute.
options.C: often measured as difference between execution price and midpoint of the bid and ask quotes at the time trade was first entered.

rationales.A: Incorrect. It is measured routinely — execution price versus the bid-ask midpoint when the trade was first entered.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Execution price relative to the entry-time midpoint is the definition.
rationales.C:
Full statement: often measured as difference between execution price and midpoint of the bid and ask quotes at the time trade was first entered; incorporates both effect of volatility/trend costs (costs of buying in rising market/selling in declining) and market impact; provides estimate of cost to execute a transaction when order is executed in a single trade.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Slippage is often measured as the difference between the execution price and the midpoint of the bid and ask quotes at the time the trade was first entered. It incorporates both the effect of volatility/trend costs and market impact. (Volatility/trend costs are the costs associated with buying in a rising market and selling in a declining market.) This measure provides an estimate of the cost to execute a transaction when the order is executed in a single trade.'

options.A: (1) slippage costs are usually more important than commission costs.
options.B: no conclusions.
options.C: no findings.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) slippage costs are usually more important than commission costs; (2) slippage costs are greater for smaller-cap securities than for large-cap securities; (3) slippage costs can vary substantially over time especially when market volatility is higher.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Exhibit 22 provides estimates of the average slippage for several markets in 2020. There are three conclusions we can draw: Slippage costs are usually more important than commission costs. Slippage costs are greater for smaller-cap securities than for large-cap securities. Slippage costs can vary substantially over time, especially when market volatility is higher.'
rationales.B: Three findings.
rationales.C: Three findings.

options.A: no such venues.
options.B: allow buyers and sellers to trade anonymously with one another.
options.C: no anonymity.

rationales.A: Dark venues.
rationales.B:
Full statement: allow buyers and sellers to trade anonymously with one another; large institutional investors today will often try to camouflage potential size of trade by breaking a trade into many smaller trades or by trading in unlit venues; dark pools and crossing networks are examples; if large institution wants to sell big block without alerting other participants, may choose to trade anonymously — unlit venues where transactions completed 'in the dark' without full transparency have become powerful force in financial markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'When a larger trade is executed in increments over multiple days, the estimate of market impact costs for later trades does not account for the impact of earlier trades on subsequent execution prices... Large institutional investors today will often try to camouflage the potential size of their trade by breaking a trade into many smaller trades or by trading in "unlit" venues. Unlit venues allow buyers and sellers to trade anonymously with one another. Dark pools and crossing networks are examples of unlit venues.' Footnote: 'If a large institution wants to sell a big block of stock but doesn't want to alert other market participants about the pending activity, it may choose to trade anonymously. Unlit venues — private trading venues where transactions are completed "in the dark" (without full transparency) — have become a powerful force in financial markets.'
rationales.C: Real venues.

drills.los_text: evaluate the efficiency of a portfolio structure given its investment mandate

options.A: no criteria.
options.B: no criteria.
options.C: risk exposures aligned with investor expectations and constraints.

rationales.A: Multiple criteria.
rationales.B: Real criteria.
rationales.C:
Full statement: risk exposures aligned with investor expectations and constraints; low idiosyncratic risk (unexplained) relative to total risk; if two products have comparable factor exposures, the product with lower absolute volatility and lower active risk will likely be preferred (assuming similar costs); if two products have similar active/absolute risks, similar costs, similar alpha skills of managers, the product having higher Active Share is preferable because it leverages alpha skills and will have higher expected returns.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In a well-constructed portfolio, we would be looking for risk exposures that are aligned with investor expectations and constraints and low idiosyncratic risk (unexplained) relative to total risk. If two products have comparable factor exposures, the product with a lower absolute volatility and lower active risk will likely be preferred (assuming similar costs). If two products have similar active and absolute risks, the portfolios have similar costs, and the alpha skills of the managers are similar, the product having a higher Active Share is preferable, because it leverages the alpha skills of the manager and will have higher expected returns.'

options.A: judged in context of investor's total portfolio.
options.B: absolute measure.
options.C: no context.

rationales.A:
Full statement: judged in context of investor's total portfolio; active risk of concentrated stock picker should be higher than that of diversified factor investor, and concentrated stock picker may have lower information ratio; yet both managers could be building well-structured portfolio relative to their mandate; important to consider diversification effect of manager's portfolio on total portfolio of investor to arrive at appropriate solution.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Finally, the "risk efficiency" of any given portfolio approach should be judged in the context of the investor's total portfolio. The active risk of a concentrated stock picker should be higher than that of a diversified factor investor, and the concentrated stock picker may have a lower information ratio. Yet both managers could be building a well-structured portfolio relative to their mandate. It is important to consider the diversification effect of a manager's portfolio on the total portfolio of the investor to arrive at an appropriate solution.'
rationales.B: Portfolio context.
rationales.C: Portfolio-level.

questions.stem: The *Exhibit 23-24 Products A & B evaluation* *most likely* shows:

options.A: Products A and B in Exhibits 23-24 are identical across every evaluated dimension — factor exposures, diversification, and risk characteristics — making the comparison uninformative for selection purposes.
options.B: Product A has Market β slightly less than 1, large-cap bias (negative Size coefficient), very large Value exposure, greater security-level diversification (~320 securities), Market risk 87.4% of total, significant portion of absolute risk attributed to Value factor.
options.C: The exhibits contain no comparative product data, presenting only a qualitative discussion of how such an evaluation might hypothetically be structured.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The products differ meaningfully — Product A shows market beta slightly under 1, a large-cap tilt, and heavy Value exposure with greater security-level diversification.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Product A has Market β slightly less than 1, large-cap bias (negative Size coefficient), very large Value exposure, greater security-level diversification (~320 securities), Market risk 87.4% of total, significant portion of absolute risk attributed to Value factor; Product B has Market β slightly more than 1, more balanced exposure to all factors, large Quality exposure, Active Share nearly double (0.80 vs 0.43), modestly lower drawdowns, ~120 securities, Product B outperformed A by more than 3.1% annually over the period.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Product A exhibits the following relevant characteristics: A Market β slightly less than 1; A large-cap bias (a negative coefficient on the Size factor); A very large exposure to the Value factor; Greater security-level diversification than Product B; Market risk representing only 87.4% of the total portfolio risk; A significant portion of the absolute risk attributed to the Value factor. The relevant characteristics for Product B are: A Market β slightly more than 1; A more balanced exposure to all factors; A large exposure to the Quality factor (although the factor itself has a relatively low volatility); Active Share nearly double that of Product A; Modestly lower drawdowns; More than 100% of its absolute risk attributed to the Market factor. Thus, Product B's emphasis on quality companies having a high return on equity, a low debt-to-equity ratio, and a low earnings variability is a likely explanation for absolute and relative risk measures that are not significantly different from those of Manager A. That Product B can achieve this level of risk efficiency with less than half the number of securities held by Product A indicates that risk management is an important component of the portfolio construction process of Product B. Although there is no guarantee that a more efficiently risk-structured portfolio will outperform, Product B outperformed Product A by more than 3.1% annually over the period.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The two-product factor and risk comparison is the substance of the exhibits.

questions.stem: *Example 8 David Larrabee custom portfolio* specifications *most likely*:

options.A: no specs.
options.B: no specs.
options.C: investable universe MSCI World.

rationales.A: Detailed specs.
rationales.B: Real specs.
rationales.C:
Full statement: investable universe MSCI World; low volatility achieved through optimization; high payout yield (dividend + share repurchase); no fewer than 250 securities; no position greater than 2%; average portfolio turnover less than 50% annually; understands low-vol objective usually leads to portfolios with large-cap, Value, and Quality biases.

Correct. Per Example 8: 'The main specifications for the custom portfolio were the following: Investable universe composed of securities within the MSCI World Index. Low volatility achieved through an optimization process. High payout yield (dividend and share repurchase). No fewer than 250 securities. No position greater than 2%. Average portfolio turnover less than 50% annually. Larrabee understands that a low-volatility objective usually leads to portfolios with large-cap, Value, and Quality biases.'

options.A: MSCI World: 7.0% return, 11.3% volatility, 1,700 securities, 2.4% turnover, 2.6% dividend yield.
options.B: no data.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: MSCI World: 7.0% return, 11.3% volatility, 1,700 securities, 2.4% turnover, 2.6% dividend yield; Custom Portfolio: 8.45% return, 9.0% volatility, 6.0% active risk, 325 securities, 35% turnover, 3.6% dividend yield; sector active exposures negative in Energy (-2.00%), Materials (-1.50%), Industrials (-1.50%) and positive in Consumer Discretionary (+3.00%), Consumer Staples (+4.20%), Health Care (+2.40%).

Correct. Per Exhibit 25 (Example 8): 'MSCI World: Return annualized 7.0%, Volatility annualized 11.3%, Number of securities 1,700, Turnover 2.4%, Dividend yield 2.6%. Custom Portfolio: Return annualized 8.45%, Volatility annualized 9.0%, Active risk 6.0%, Number of securities 325, Turnover 35%, Dividend yield 3.6%. Average Active Sector Exposure — Energy: -2.00%, Materials: -1.50%, Industrials: -1.50%, Consumer discretionary: 3.00%, Consumer staples: 4.20%, Health care: 2.40%.'
rationales.B: Detailed results.
rationales.C: Real data.

options.A: no concern.
options.B: 6% active risk on 65% of equity allocation may be too high.
options.C: no issue.

rationales.A: Recommendations.
rationales.B:
Full statement: 6% active risk on 65% of equity allocation may be too high; some tracking error attributed to significant under-allocation to information technology sector; excess return achieved largely through very high and intentional exposure to rewarded factors (Value, BAB, Quality) which may not have been rewarded over simulated period; alpha is negative — understanding source of this negative alpha is essential.

Correct. Per Example 8 solution: 'Given the size of this allocation relative to the total equity portfolio, this poses a problem. Some of this tracking error may be attributed to a significant under-allocation to the information technology sector. Finally, although the portfolio would have generated an excess return on average over the past 12 years, the alpha is negative. Understanding the source of this negative alpha is essential. In this instance, the excess return was achieved largely through a very high and intentional exposure to rewarded factors, such as Value, BAB, and Quality, which may not have been rewarded over the simulated period.'
rationales.C: Concerns to address.

options.A: no recommendations.
options.B: no advice.
options.C: first investigate further source of significant negative alpha.

rationales.A: Three recommendations.
rationales.B: Real advice.
rationales.C:
Full statement: first investigate further source of significant negative alpha; if tracking error is concern, identify satellite managers whose active returns have low correlation with core mandate perhaps even lower active risk; considering importance of information technology sector, could be prudent to hire manager with strong technology orientation — objective not to maintain technology exposure equal to MSCI World but to lower consistent underexposure to more reasonable level; structural biases should be continuously monitored.

Correct. Per Example 8 second solution: 'The first recommendation would be to investigate further the source of the significant negative alpha. Because the excess performance is so strongly explained by exposure to specific factors, we should be concerned about how the portfolio would perform if factor returns were to decline. Is there a systemic reason that can explain this observation? Secondly, if tracking error is a concern, it is important to identify satellite managers whose active returns have a low correlation with the core mandate, perhaps even a lower active risk. Finally, considering the importance of the information technology sector, it could be prudent to hire a manager that has a strong technology orientation. The objective is not necessarily to maintain a technology exposure equal to that of the MSCI World Index but perhaps to lower the consistent underexposure to a more reasonable level. At the very least, these structural biases should be continuously monitored.'

rationales.B:

Source of risk: Market 69.2% (Significantly less exposure to the Market factor, consistent with a concentrated, high-Active-Share manager). Sectors 11.6% (Significantly more Sector risk). Styles 9.7% (Significantly more Style risk). Unexplained 9.5% (Significantly higher proportion of risk is unexplained). Taken together, these measures indicate a benchmark-agnostic strategy with significant and concentrated security, sector, and style exposures.'

drills.los_text: discuss the long-only, long extension, long/short, and equitized market-neutral approaches to equity portfolio construction, including their risks, costs, and effects on potential alphas

options.A: single strategy.
options.B: no relationship.
options.C: 'long/short' is the most encompassing term and can include long extension and market-neutral products.

rationales.A: Umbrella term.
rationales.B: Encompassing term.
rationales.C:
Full statement: 'long/short' is the most encompassing term and can include long extension and market-neutral products; commonly refers to strategies that are relatively unconstrained in the extent to which they can lever both positive and negative insights; each is predicated on belief that research insights can be exploited not only in pursuit of stocks expected to perform well but also to profit from negative insights.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Long/short, long extension, and market-neutral portfolio approaches are all variations on a theme: Each is predicated on the belief that research insights can be exploited not only in the pursuit of stocks that are expected to perform well but also to profit from the negative insights gathered during the research process. "Long/short" is the most encompassing term and can include long extension and market-neutral products. Most commonly, the term "long/short" refers to strategies that are relatively unconstrained in the extent to which they can lever both positive and negative insights.'

options.A: constrained long/short strategies — capital committed by client invested similarly to long-only strategy but levered to some extent to exploit manager's insights on projected losers as well as winners.
options.B: Long extension strategies use no leverage, holding long positions equal to capital only.
options.C: The reading provides no framework for long extension strategies.

rationales.A:
Full statement: constrained long/short strategies — capital committed by client invested similarly to long-only strategy but levered to some extent to exploit manager's insights on projected losers as well as winners; typical long-extension is constrained to have net exposure of 100% (e.g., 130% long and 30% short, net 100% — same as long-only).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Long extension strategies are constrained long/short strategies. rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) unlike long position, short position moves against manager if price of security increases; (2) L/S strategies sometimes require significant leverage; (3) cost of borrowing security can become prohibitive particularly if security is hard to borrow; (4) collateral requirements increase if short position moves against manager — in extreme, manager may be forced to liquidate favorably-ranked long positions and short positions eventually reversing; short squeeze possible (price of shorted stock rises so much and so quickly that many short investors unable to maintain positions in short run in light of increased collateral requirements — 'squeeze' worsened as short-sellers liquidate).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Strategies that short securities contain the following inherent risks, which must be understood: 1. Unlike a long position, a short position will move against the manager if the price of the security increases. 2. Long/short strategies sometimes require significant leverage. Leverage must be used wisely. 3. The cost of borrowing a security can become prohibitive, particularly if the security is hard to borrow. 4. Collateral requirements will increase if a short position moves against the manager. In extreme cases, the manager may be forced to liquidate some favorably ranked long positions (and short positions that might eventually reverse) if too much leverage has been used. The manager may also fall victim to a short squeeze. A short squeeze is a situation in which the price of the stock that has been shorted has risen so much and so quickly that many short investors may be unable to maintain their positions in the short run in light of the increased collateral requirements. The "squeeze" is worsened as short-sellers liquidate their short position, buying back the security and possibly pushing the price even higher.'
rationales.B: Four risks.
rationales.C: Four risks.

rationales.A: Detailed rules.
rationales.B:
Full statement: prime broker requires collateral to assure lenders that contracts will be honored; higher relative amount of short-selling means greater collateral required; portfolio with 20% capital invested short may be required to put up collateral equal to 40% of short positions, whereas 100% short portfolio could be required to put up 200% of short positions; different asset types weighed differently (US Treasury bill may be 100% of value toward required collateral; high-yield bond or restricted-liquidity asset only portion of market value counts).

Correct. Per the reading: 'As previously indicated, to short-sell securities, investors typically rely on a prime broker who can help them locate the securities they wish to borrow. But the prime broker will require collateral from the short-sellers to assure the lenders of these securities that their contracts will be honored. The higher the relative amount of short-selling in a portfolio, the greater the amount of collateral required. A portfolio with 20% of capital invested short may be required to put up collateral equal to 40% of the short positions, whereas a portfolio with 100% of capital invested short could be required to put up collateral equal to 200% of the short positions. In addition, different types of assets are weighed differently in the calculation of collateral value. For example, a US Treasury bill may be viewed as very safe collateral and accorded 100% of its value toward the required collateral. In contrast, a high-yield bond or some other asset with restricted liquidity would have only a portion of its market value counted toward the collateral requirement.'
rationales.C: Real requirements.

rationales.C:
Full statement: equal-risk-premium products seek to extract return premiums from rewarded factors often across asset classes — manager creates L/S sub-portfolios extracting premiums (Size, Value, Momentum, Low Beta) and combines using weightings ensuring each contributes same amount of risk to overall portfolio; combination may be levered across all sub-portfolios to achieve specific volatility level; equal-risk-premium products (removing Market factor effect) often need three units of leverage long and short to achieve 10% absolute risk target; investor is not abandoning equity-like returns and is using market-neutral portfolio as an overlay.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A comprehensive use of long/short strategies can also be found in the design of equal-risk-premium products. Such products seek to extract return premiums from rewarded factors, often across asset classes. To do so, the manager must create long/short sub-portfolios extracting these premiums (such as Size, Value, Momentum, and Low Beta) and combine these sub-portfolios using weightings that ensure each component will contribute the same amount of risk to the overall portfolio. The combination may be levered across all sub-portfolios to achieve a specific volatility level.' And also: 'equal-risk-premium products (that remove the effect of the Market factor) often need three units of leverage long and short to achieve a 10% absolute risk target.' Equitized approach: 'exposure and overlay long/short strategies. In this case, the investor is not abandoning equity-like returns and is using the market-neutral portfolio as an overlay.'
